literal: gradation, ranking; abstract noun from tara-tama (more-most)
invariant_definition: Gradation — Madhva's distinctive doctrine that *jīva*s and even *muktas* are intrinsically ranked. *Tāratamya* is one of the most original and most often misread of Madhva's doctrines: souls are not equal in their constitutive natures, even after liberation. The *taratamya* hierarchy runs from Lakṣmī (highest *paratantra*) down through Brahmā, Vāyu, and the senior gods to the human *muktas* and on through the *nitya-saṃsārī* class to the *tamo-yogya* class fated for *andha-tamas*. *Tāratamya* is metaphysically primitive: not a function of *karma*, not a phase of cosmic evolution, but the eternal structure of the *paratantra* tattvas.

per_school.definition: *Tāratamya* is intrinsic, eternal, and primitive. It applies even in *mokṣa*: liberated souls enjoy *ānanda* in proportion to their constitutive nature (*ānanda-taratamya*), and equality of liberated souls is denied. Madhva's *Mahābhārata-Tātparya-Nirṇaya* devotes extensive treatment to the gradation of major figures (Bhīma > Arjuna > Yudhiṣṭhira; Vāyu as the highest *jīva*; etc.).

Strongly rejected. Advaita: all *jīva*s are the one *ātman*, equality at the *pāramārthika* level. Viśiṣṭādvaita: liberated souls are equal in *bhoga* of *brahman*. Acintya-Bhedābheda: gradation is by *rasa* (the *bhakta*'s relational mode), not by intrinsic nature.

translator_note: 'Hierarchy' or 'gradation' acceptable. Note: Madhva's *taratamya* is metaphysically realist — souls *really differ* in constitutive worth. Western readers will find this jarring; the user's project explicitly forbids softening it.
